text: Last class: they are direct, whereas we build rapport first.
Today: we have closed two deals, revenue has risen, and each party shall deliver on time.

They are direct, whereas we build rapport; their pace is different from ours.
Maria Claudia, we have built a strong track record together. Shall we open this final round with the numbers?
Yes. Revenue has risen sharply this quarter, while costs stayed flat. Each party shall deliver on time, and the contract must include a twelve-month warranty.
Agreed. We have two options: a lower price now, or a longer contract. The longer one is a fair trade-off. We had better confirm the deadline today.
I agree. Your pace is direct, whereas ours is slower; your urgency is different from ours. I will adapt my mindset and state my point early.
And keep your warmth. A short comment on art still builds rapport -- our taste is similar to yours, after all.
Perfect. We have a strong track record, clear terms, and a fair trade-off. I am ready to close this deal.
Revenue has risen sharply this quarter.
We had better confirm the deadline today.

Maria Claudia, congratulations on reaching the end of Block Two. Before our final call, here is a quick briefing that uses everything you have studied. Over the last weeks we have closed two deals together, and we have built a strong track record. For tomorrow's negotiation, a few points. First, the terms: each party shall deliver on time, and the contract must include a twelve-month warranty; either side may request a small change before we sign. Second, the numbers: revenue has risen sharply this quarter, whereas costs have stayed flat, so our position is strong. Third, my advice: we should open with the data, and we had better confirm the deadline early, because the buyer is in a hurry. One more thing on culture. Their style is direct, unlike our slower warm-up, so state your main point near the start. Their taste in numbers is similar to ours, but their sense of urgency is different from ours. Keep your warm mindset, add American directness, and you will close this beautifully. See you tomorrow.

It is the end of Block Two, and I want to look back honestly at how far I have come. When I started, the passive voice and my prepositions were weak. Now I can describe a track record with the present perfect: we have closed three deals, and revenue has grown. I can talk about obligations with modals: each party shall deliver, the contract must include a warranty, and either side may request a change. I can give advice that sounds professional: we should weigh the options, and we had better decide before the deadline. I can describe trends with the right verbs: sales rose sharply, then plateaued. And I can compare two cultures fairly: they are direct, whereas we build rapport first; their etiquette is different from ours, never different of ours. My goal for tomorrow is simple. I will lead the negotiation in clear, confident English, I will keep my own custom of warmth, and I will adapt my mindset where it helps. Block Two is complete, and I am ready.

First, you report your track record with the present perfect, for example we have closed three deals this quarter. Then, you state the obligations with modals: each party shall deliver, and the contract must include a warranty. After that, you present the numbers with trend language, such as revenue has risen sharply while costs have stayed flat. Next, you give a clear recommendation with should or had better, for example we should open with the data, and we had better confirm the deadline. Finally, you compare the two cultures fairly with whereas and unlike, so you adapt your approach and still keep your own strengths.
